channel.title: News & Events | TMU Multimedia Research Laboratory

item.title: Youth Reflection: Kids Help Phone AI Training Pilot
item.description:
Kids Help Phone youth councillor Simran has written a reflection piece on RML’s recent pilot project conducted in partnership with Kids Help Phone — exploring the development of an agentic AI framework for training frontline support workers.

The piece offers a youth perspective on how AI-assisted training tools could improve the consistency and quality of mental health support for young people. Read the full reflection: “One Session, Infinite Support: A Youth Perspective on Mental Health Research”

item.title: NSERC Alliance Grant Awarded for AI-Assisted Intestinal Ultrasound Analysis
item.description:
Dr. Naimul Khan has been awarded an NSERC Alliance grant (with Mitacs Accelerate support) totalling $120K to develop AI algorithms for segmentation and analysis of intestinal ultrasound, in partnership with Dova Health Intelligence.

The project targets faster and more accurate assessment of Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) — a condition affecting millions of Canadians — by automating the interpretation of intestinal ultrasound images using deep learning. IBD assessment currently relies heavily on specialist expertise, and AI-assisted tools have the potential to significantly reduce diagnostic delays.

This project is led by Dr. Randy Tan as post-doctoral research lead.

item.title: Bronze Medal at Kaggle — Bacterial Flagellar Motors Detection
item.description:
MASc student Yufei Ren and Dr. Naimul Khan won a Bronze Medal in the Kaggle BYU Locating Bacterial Flagellar Motors in 3D Tomograms competition, placing 64th out of 1,136 teams.

The task involved detecting bacterial flagellar motor locations in 3D cryo-electron tomograms — a challenging volumetric detection problem with significant implications for structural biology. Our approach was based on MHAF-YOLO, a multi-head attention feature fusion architecture adapted for 3D detection.

Congratulations Yufei!

item.title: RML at IEEE ISBI 2025
item.description:
RML will be presenting at the IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) 2025, showcasing the latest progress on AI-guided neonatal cardiac ultrasound analysis.

Paper: Temporal Feature Weaving for Neonatal Echocardiographic Viewpoint Video Classification S. French, F. Zhu, A. Jain, N. Khan

The work introduces a novel temporal feature weaving approach for automatically classifying echocardiographic viewpoints in neonatal ultrasound video, a critical step toward fully automated cardiac assessment in the NICU.

item.title: RML at IEEE EMBC 2024
item.description:
RML members presented three papers at the 46th Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBC 2024). Congratulations to Behzad and Paolo!

  • EEG Signal Denoising Using Beta-Variational Autoencoder B. Mahaseni, N. Khan

  • Multi-modal Emotion Recognition Using EEG and Eye Tracking Features P. Iacono, N. Khan

  • Structure Preserving CycleGAN for Unsupervised Medical Image Domain Adaptation P. Iacono, N. Khan

item.title: New Frontiers in Research Fund Award: Therapeutic VR for Children on the Spectrum
item.description:
Dr. Naimul Khan has been awarded a $250,000 New Frontiers in Research Fund (NFRF) Exploration Award to develop dynamic therapeutic VR experiences for children on the autism spectrum.

The project is a collaboration with Wellwave Inc. and the Holland Bloorview Kids Rehabilitation Hospital — Canada’s largest children’s rehabilitation hospital. The research will focus on creating adaptive, personalized VR interventions that respond to real-time physiological and behavioural sensing to maximize therapeutic benefit.

item.title: Best Paper Runner-Up at CVPR 2021 Workshop
item.description:
Congratulations to Julian True! His paper “Motion Vector Extrapolation for Video Object Detection” won the Best Paper Runner-Up Award at the CVPR 2021 Robust Video Understanding Workshop.

The paper demonstrates that leveraging motion vectors from compressed video can decrease object detection latency by up to 25× while maintaining detection accuracy — a significant advance for real-time video understanding on resource-constrained devices.

item.title: New Frontiers in Research Fund Award: De-escalation VR Training for Emergency Responders
item.description:
Dr. Naimul Khan has been awarded a $250,000 New Frontiers in Research Fund (NFRF) Exploration Award to develop a virtual reality de-escalation training platform for Canada’s emergency responders.

In partnership with the Ontario Provincial Police, the project will create adaptive scenario-based VR training experiences using multimodal sensing to dynamically respond to trainee behaviour in real time. The interdisciplinary team includes:

  • Dr. Natalie Alvarez (School of Performing Arts) — narrative design and embodied learning
  • Dr. Jennifer Lavoie (Criminology, Wilfrid Laurier University) — crisis intervention expertise

item.title: NSERC Alliance Grant: Therapeutic VR Games for Children
item.description:
Dr. Naimul Khan has been awarded a $400,000 NSERC Alliance Grant in partnership with Wellwave Inc.

The project will focus on developing therapeutic virtual reality games with integrated multimodal stress assessment for children. By continuously sensing physiological and behavioural responses, the system adapts game content in real time to support stress regulation and therapeutic outcomes.

item.title: RML at IEEE EMBC 2020
item.description:
RML members will present two papers at the 42nd Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBC 2020).

Both papers report the latest results from the lab’s ongoing collaboration with Shaftesbury Inc., focusing on stress assessment from multimodal physiological sensors using machine learning. The work explores fusion of ECG, EEG, and behavioural signals to build robust, real-time stress classifiers for VR-based therapeutic applications.

item.title: NSERC Alliance Grant: AI-Guided Neonatal Ultrasound Diagnosis and Training
item.description:
Dr. Ling Guan, Dr. Naimul Khan, and Dr. Karthi Umapathy have been awarded a $669,000 NSERC Alliance Grant in partnership with Dapasoft Inc.

The project will develop interpretable machine learning algorithms for computer-aided diagnosis of neonatal lung and heart diseases. Mount Sinai Hospital’s Neonatal Intensive Care Unit — the largest NICU in Canada — relies on point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) as the primary imaging modality for cardiac and pulmonary disease diagnosis in neonates. Operating POCUS on neonates requires a highly specialized skillset, and the project will additionally develop AI-guided training tools to accelerate proficiency development for new ultrasound technologists.

item.title: NSERC CRD Award: Cloud-based Stress and Engagement Assessment in VR
item.description:
Dr. Naimul Khan (principal investigator), Dr. Ling Guan, and Dr. Sridhar Krishnan have received a $450,000 NSERC Collaborative Research and Development Grant in partnership with Shaftesbury Inc.

The project, titled “Cloud-based Machine Learning Framework for Assessment of Stress/Engagement Through Multimodal Sensors”, will develop machine learning and signal processing algorithms to assess stress and engagement levels within VR applications. The assessment results will be used to dynamically adjust game content in real time — with applications in:

  • Clinical settings: non-pharmacological anxiety reduction before surgery
  • Entertainment: dynamic content adaptation for personalized gaming experiences

item.title: NSERC CRD Award: IoT Signals for Medical Emergency Decision-Making
item.description:
Dr. Ling Guan and Dr. Naimul Khan have been awarded a $300,000 NSERC Collaborative Research and Development Grant in partnership with Dapasoft Inc.

This two-year project will develop new information processing tools and techniques to enhance emergency medical services. The system will utilize IoT sensors to continuously record patient symptoms, with machine learning analysis and immersive context-aware visualization methods to assist healthcare professionals in timely diagnosis and decision-making in emergency situations.

item.title: NSERC CRD Award: Intelligent Scene Understanding for Mobile AR
item.description:
Dr. Naimul Khan has been awarded a $90,000 NSERC Collaborative Research and Development Grant in partnership with AWE Company Ltd.

This one-year project will develop machine learning algorithms that leverage point cloud data from modern mobile AR libraries (ARKit/ARCore) to provide intelligent 3D scene understanding. The resulting scene understanding capabilities will further enable collaborative augmented reality experiences between multiple users sharing the same physical space.

item.title: NSERC Engage Grant: Multimodal Stress Assessment for Pediatric VR
item.description:
Dr. Naimul Khan has been awarded a $25,000 NSERC Engage grant in partnership with Shaftesbury VR.

The project will develop a multimodal stress assessment algorithm to be integrated into Shaftesbury’s Positive Distraction Entertainment System — a platform designed to dynamically adapt VR game content to reduce anxiety in children preparing for medical procedures. By fusing physiological and behavioural signals, the system aims to provide a non-pharmacological approach to pre-procedural anxiety reduction.

item.title: Best Paper Award at IEEE ISM 2017
item.description:
Congratulations to Randy Tan! His paper on real-time human activity analysis won the Best Paper Award at the IEEE International Symposium on Multimedia (ISM) 2017, Workshop on Quality of Experience in Visual Media Processing and Transmission.

The work demonstrates a real-time system for recognizing and analyzing human activities from video, with applications in surveillance, healthcare monitoring, and interactive media.

item.title: NSERC CRD Award: 3D Object Recognition for Museum Exhibits
item.description:
Dr. Naimul Khan, Dr. Ling Guan, and Dr. Andy Ye have been awarded a $375,000 NSERC Collaborative Research and Development Grant in partnership with SimentIT Inc.

This two-year project will develop novel 3D object recognition and tracking algorithms for museum augmented reality exhibits. The technology will be deployed at the newly renovated Canada Science and Technology Museum in Ottawa, enabling visitors to experience interactive AR overlays on physical artifacts.
